svg.g: Implementing an Ethernet LAN using n Nodes Local Area Network (LAN): A computer network that spans a small geographical area, typically within a building or campus. Ethernet: A family of local area networking technologies that use cables, and, typically, a coaxial cable or twisted pair of copper wires. Traffic Node: A node that receives and sends data packets in an Ethernet network. Congestion Window: The amount of data that can be transmitted between two nodes without causing network congestion. Setup of the LAN: Connect each node to a switch or a hub using Ethernet cables. Make sure each node is connected to a different port on the switch or hub. Configure the switch or hub to assign IP addresses to each node. You can use a dynamic host configuration protocol (DHCP) server or manually assign IP addresses. Install NICs in each node to enable network communication. Configure the network settings on each node, including the IP address, subnet mask, gateway, and DNS server. Multiple Traffic Nodes: Create multiple traffic nodes by assigning different IP addresses to each node. Configure the traffic routing on each node to forward packets to the destination node. Monitor the traffic between each node to ensure that packets are being transmitted correctly. Requirements: Include SVG dimensions in the XML header. Include data-topic-id attribute with slugified topic name. Use gradient background with pastel colors. Include 4-6 sections with data-narration attributes explaining each part. <text x="0" y="710" font-size="18" font-family="Arial" fill="#2ecc71" data-narration="5. Add smooth animations using elements with sequential begin times">Add smooth animations using elements with sequential begin times. Use professional color scheme (indigo, teal, orange accents). Include clear labels and annotations. Add arrow markers for connections. Include a title at top and footer at bottom. Visualizing Congestion Window
